learning_objectives: Describe the technical architecture of modern LMS platforms
Configure course settings, enrollment, and permissions in an LMS
Compare open-source and commercial LMS platforms

lecture_notes.introduction: Learning Management Systems are the backbone of digital education infrastructure. Understanding how they work under the hood — not just as an instructor, but as an administrator — is essential for educational technologists who will select, configure, and manage these platforms.
key_concepts: LMS architecture: database, application layer, file storage, API layer
Role-based access control: admin, instructor, TA, student, observer
Course configuration: enrollment methods, grade schemes, module release conditions
Open-source (Moodle, Canvas open) vs. commercial (Blackboard, D2L Brightspace)
Total cost of ownership: licensing, hosting, support, customization, training
lecture_notes.demonstration: LMS administration walkthrough: configure a course from scratch in Canvas including enrollment, modules, grade setup, and permissions

review.value: RBAC ensures users see only what they need: admins manage the platform, instructors manage courses, TAs grade, students submit — misconfigured permissions cause data breaches or grade errors
Free open-source LMS software still costs money: hosting, maintenance, support, and customization. A proper TCO analysis compares 5-year costs including all hidden expenses.

class_schedule.topic: LTI, APIs, and the LMS Ecosystem
learning_objectives: Explain how LTI integrations connect external tools to an LMS
Use LMS APIs to automate administrative tasks
Design an integrated learning technology ecosystem

lecture_notes.introduction: No LMS does everything. The modern learning technology ecosystem connects the LMS to external tools — video platforms, plagiarism checkers, publishers, proctoring, analytics — through standards like LTI and REST APIs.
key_concepts: LTI 1.3: secure tool integration with grade passback
REST APIs: programmatic access to LMS data and functions
SIS integration: syncing enrollment from Student Information Systems
Content standards: SCORM, xAPI, QTI, Common Cartridge
Ecosystem design: choosing tools that interoperate vs. monolithic solutions
lecture_notes.demonstration: API automation exercise: use the Canvas API to bulk-create assignment groups, demonstrating how automation reduces repetitive administrative tasks
setup_requirements: Postman or curl for API calls

review.value: The current standard for securely connecting external tools to an LMS; supports single sign-on, grade passback, and deep linking — replacing the less secure LTI 1.1
Rather than selecting one tool that does everything poorly, design an ecosystem of best-of-breed tools connected through LTI and APIs

learning_objectives: Implement consistent, accessible course templates in an LMS
Design effective navigation and content organization patterns
Apply Quality Matters standards to LMS course design
readings: Canvas/Moodle course design best practices documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: A well-designed LMS course reduces cognitive load, supports self-directed learning, and ensures accessibility. This session covers repeatable design patterns that work across platforms.
key_concepts: Module templates: consistent structure reduces navigation confusion
Homepage design: orientation, navigation, and first impressions
Content pages vs. assignments vs. discussions: choosing the right LMS tool
Conditional release: prerequisite-based content unlocking
Master course and course copy workflows for multi-section management
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a course template: create a reusable module template with consistent structure (overview, content, activity, assessment, reflection) that can be copied across courses
setup_requirements: LMS sandbox with course creation permissions

review.value: Unlocking content based on prerequisites (completed a quiz, viewed a page) guides students through a deliberate learning path and prevents skipping foundational material
Create one master course, refine it, then copy to individual sections. Updates to the master propagate to copies, ensuring consistency across multiple sections.

learning_objectives: Configure complex grading schemes in an LMS gradebook
Design rubrics, outcomes, and competency-based grading in LMS
readings: Feldman, J. - Grading for Equity Ch. 7 (LMS implementation)

lecture_notes.introduction: The gradebook is the most consequential feature of any LMS. Misconfigured grading can produce incorrect grades that affect student GPAs, financial aid, and transcripts. This session covers both the technical setup and the pedagogical choices embedded in gradebook design.
key_concepts: Grading schemes: points, percentages, weighted categories, competency-based
Assignment groups and weighting calculations
Rubric creation: criteria, ratings, and outcome alignment
Late policies, lowest-score dropping, and grade override mechanisms
Common errors: ungraded assignments, muted columns, missing policy settings
lecture_notes.demonstration: Gradebook configuration lab: set up a weighted gradebook with assignment groups, late penalty policies, lowest quiz drop, and a rubric-graded assignment

review.value: Most common source of gradebook errors: ensure weights sum to 100%, ungraded items are properly excluded, and the 'treat ungraded as zero' setting matches your pedagogical intent
Feldman argues for minimum grading (50% floor), most-recent-score for mastery, and separating academic achievement from behavior in the gradebook — all configurable in modern LMS platforms

learning_objectives: Extract and interpret learning analytics from LMS data
Design dashboards for different stakeholders (instructors, admins, students)
Address privacy and ethical concerns in LMS data use
readings: Slade, S. & Prinsloo, P. - Learning Analytics: Ethical Issues and Dilemmas

lecture_notes.introduction: LMS platforms collect rich behavioral data: when students log in, what they access, how long they spend, what they submit, and how they perform. Turning this data into actionable insights requires both technical and ethical competence.
key_concepts: Available LMS data: access logs, submission times, grade distributions, discussion activity
Instructor dashboards: identifying at-risk students, content engagement patterns
Institutional reporting: course completion rates, program outcomes, accreditation data
Student-facing analytics: progress tracking, self-regulation support
FERPA and privacy: what data can be shared, with whom, and under what conditions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analytics investigation: given anonymized LMS data for a course, identify three at-risk students and three course design improvements supported by the data

review.value: Data is only useful if it leads to action. A dashboard showing 'Student X hasn't logged in for 7 days' is actionable. A report showing 'average page views per student' is interesting but rarely leads to specific interventions.
Student education records are protected. LMS data shared with third-party tools must comply with FERPA; institutional data governance policies must be in place before integrating analytics tools.

class_schedule.topic: LMS Migration, Selection, and Future Trends
learning_objectives: Develop an LMS evaluation and selection framework
Plan an LMS migration project including data, content, and training
Evaluate emerging trends in learning platform technology
readings: EDUCAUSE Review: The State of the LMS
Phil Hill - State of Higher Ed LMS Market analysis

lecture_notes.introduction: LMS selection and migration are among the highest-stakes technology decisions an educational institution makes. The process affects every instructor and student. This session covers how to do it well.
key_concepts: LMS selection criteria: features, integration, accessibility, support, cost, scalability
Stakeholder involvement: faculty, students, IT, administration, disability services
Migration planning: content export/import, grade history, training, communication
Pilot and phased rollout strategies
Future trends: unbundled LMS, AI-native platforms, competency-based platforms
lecture_notes.demonstration: LMS selection simulation: role-play a selection committee evaluating three LMS finalists, each team representing a different stakeholder group
setup_requirements: LMS comparison dossiers for three platforms

review.value: The biggest risk in LMS migration is not technical but human: faculty resistance, training gaps, and loss of content. Communication and support are more important than the technology itself.
Emerging trend: instead of one monolithic LMS, institutions assemble a stack of best-of-breed tools connected through LTI and APIs, with the LMS serving as a hub rather than a container.

quizzes.topic: LMS Architecture, APIs, and Course Design

questions.question: Explain the difference between LTI 1.1 and LTI 1.3. Why was the upgrade necessary, and what new capabilities does LTI 1.3 provide?
questions.answer: LTI 1.1 used OAuth 1.0 for authentication, which had known security vulnerabilities and sent consumer secrets in plain text. LTI 1.3 uses OAuth 2.0 with public/private key pairs, eliminating shared secrets. New capabilities: (1) improved security through asymmetric cryptography, (2) deep linking for content selection workflows, (3) Names and Roles Provisioning Service for accessing course rosters, (4) Assignment and Grade Services for richer grade passback. The upgrade was necessary because the security model of 1.1 was inadequate for modern data protection requirements.

rubric.key: Explains why the upgrade was necessary

questions.question: A faculty member reports that student grades in the LMS are lower than expected. Upon investigation, you find that the gradebook has 'treat ungraded as zero' enabled and three assignments are still unmuted. Explain the impact of each setting and how to fix the situation.
questions.answer: Impact of 'treat ungraded as zero': assignments that have not yet been graded (or that students have not yet submitted because the deadline has not passed) are counted as zeros in the grade calculation, artificially deflating all student grades. Fix: disable this setting until all assignments are past due and graded, or use it only at end of term. Impact of muted assignments: grades exist in the system but are hidden from students and may or may not be included in grade calculations depending on the LMS. Fix: unmute assignments after grading is complete so grades are visible and properly calculated. Prevention: establish a gradebook setup checklist that addresses these settings before the course opens.

rubric.key: Correctly explains impact of muted assignments
Provides actionable fixes and prevention strategies

questions.question: An institution is considering migrating from Blackboard to Canvas. Identify the three most critical risk factors in this migration and describe one mitigation strategy for each.
questions.answer: (1) Content loss: not all Blackboard content formats export cleanly to Canvas (especially test banks, custom page layouts, and embedded media). Mitigation: conduct a content audit before migration, identify high-risk content types, and manually rebuild critical content rather than relying solely on automated import. (2) Faculty resistance: instructors who have invested years in Blackboard will resist learning a new platform. Mitigation: involve faculty champions early, provide hands-on training workshops (not just documentation), and maintain parallel access to Blackboard during the transition period. (3) Grade history: historical grade data may not transfer completely. Mitigation: archive all Blackboard gradebooks as CSV exports before migration, maintain read-only Blackboard access for one academic year, and communicate to registrar about the transition.

rubric.key: Identifies three distinct, critical risk factors
Mitigation strategies are specific and practical
Demonstrates understanding of both technical and human challenges

questions.question: What is the difference between student-facing analytics and instructor-facing analytics in an LMS? Give one example of each and explain how each supports learning.
questions.answer: Student-facing analytics present data to students about their own learning behavior and performance to support self-regulation. Example: a progress dashboard showing 'You have completed 8 of 12 modules, your average quiz score is 85%, and you have 2 assignments due this week.' This supports learning by helping students monitor their own progress, identify areas needing attention, and plan their time. Instructor-facing analytics present aggregated or individual student data to instructors to support teaching decisions. Example: a heatmap showing which content pages students access most and which they skip, combined with item analysis showing which quiz questions have the lowest scores. This supports learning by enabling the instructor to identify confusing content, provide targeted review, and reach out to struggling students.

rubric.key: Clear distinction between the two types
Student-facing example with learning support explanation
Instructor-facing example with teaching support explanation

major_assignments.title: LMS Course Build and Administration Project
major_assignments.description: Build a complete, functional course in an LMS platform (Canvas or Moodle). The course must include: a homepage with orientation content, 4 weekly modules with consistent structure, a configured gradebook with weighted categories, at least one LTI integration, rubric-graded assignments, and a student analytics dashboard. Submit a walkthrough screencast and an administration guide.
objectives: Configure a complete LMS course with professional-grade settings
Implement LTI integrations and API-based automation
Design an accessible, well-organized course structure
Create administrative documentation for course maintenance
deliverables: Functional LMS course (shared access or exported package)
Administration guide (2,000 words) covering configuration, maintenance, and troubleshooting
Gradebook configuration documentation with grade calculation examples

criteria: Complete gradebook with correct weighted categories
Enrollment and permission settings properly configured
At least one LTI tool integrated and functional
Consistent module structure with clear navigation
Effective use of LMS features (discussions, quizzes, rubrics)
Clear demonstration of all required features

quizzes.description: Bi-weekly quizzes on LMS administration, APIs, and analytics

lab_assignments.description: Weekly labs: course configuration, API exercises, gradebook setup, analytics interpretation

major_assignments.description: LMS course build and administration project

midterm_examination.description: Covers LMS architecture, LTI/API integration, and course design patterns

final_project.description: LMS migration plan or ecosystem design project with stakeholder analysis

course_policies.attendance: Lab sessions are mandatory for hands-on LMS configuration work. More than 2 unexcused absences will reduce the final grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work loses 5% per day for up to 5 days. LMS sandbox access expires at semester end; all work must be completed before then.
course_policies.collaboration: Peer review of LMS courses is required. All course builds and documentation must be individually produced.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All course content in your LMS build must be original or properly attributed. Violations result in a zero and referral.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Computer with internet access. LMS sandbox accounts provided by the institution. Postman or curl for API exercises.
